_meta.game: Super Mario Bros.: The Lost Levels
_meta.region: Japan (Famicom Disk System) / North America (SNES All-Stars rerelease)

sources.name: TASVideos — NES / Super Mario Bros 2 (Japan)
sources.notes: Uses exact SMB1 engine. Luigi differences and wind mechanics documented.

sources.name: Wikipedia — Super Mario Bros.: The Lost Levels
sources.notes: Luigi physics confirmed: higher jump, lower traction.

notes: Originally released in Japan as Super Mario Bros. 2 (FDS, 1986). Not released in North America until Super Mario All-Stars (1993).
USES THE EXACT SAME ENGINE AS SMB1. All physics constants are identical for Mario.
KEY ADDITION: Luigi has different physics — higher jump (lower gravity while holding jump) but more slippery (less ground friction).
KEY ADDITION: Wind zones. Some levels have wind that pushes Mario/Luigi horizontally or vertically.
KEY ADDITION: Poison mushrooms — look like mushrooms but damage the player.
KEY ADDITION: Warp zones that send the player backward (anti-warps) as traps.
This is NOT the American 'Super Mario Bros. 2' (which is Doki Doki Panic). Designed as a hardcore difficulty sequel.

mario_physics._note: Mario's physics are IDENTICAL to SMB1. All values from SMB1 documentation apply.

gravity_holding_jump.notes: Reduced gravity when holding jump button — the mechanism for variable height.

luigi_physics._note: Luigi has DIFFERENT physics from Mario. Deliberate design to distinguish the characters.

jump_height_modifier.description: Luigi jumps higher than Mario. Lower gravity applied when holding jump button.
jump_height_modifier.notes: Luigi can reach platforms Mario cannot with the same input.

ground_friction_modifier.description: Luigi has less ground friction (more slippery). Takes longer to decelerate and has more slide when stopping.
ground_friction_modifier.notes: The reduced traction makes Luigi harder to control precisely — a trade-off for the higher jump.

practical_difference: Luigi is preferred for reaching high platforms or long-distance jumps
Mario is preferred for precise platform landing (less slide)
Luigi on ice or moving platforms requires extra caution due to compounded slipperiness

wind_mechanic._note: New mechanic not in SMB1. Wind zones apply horizontal or vertical forces to the player.

horizontal_wind.description: Pushes Mario/Luigi left or right. Can work with or against movement direction.
horizontal_wind.effect: Modifies horizontal velocity — adds to or subtracts from player's own momentum.

vertical_wind.description: Pushes player up or down while airborne.
vertical_wind.effect: Modifies jump arc — can make jumps go higher or shorter than expected.

notes: Wind is invisible — players must learn its presence from experience or positioning cues.
Wind interacts with standard physics — it adds to/from the existing velocity, not replaces it.
Critical to speedrun routing — wind-assisted sections allow faster movement.
The invisible nature of wind forces players to adapt their jumping intuition per-level.

poison_mushroom.description: Appears to be a power-up mushroom but damages the player on contact.
poison_mushroom.visual: Resembles a standard mushroom but with a different color (darker/reddish)
poison_mushroom.notes: A trap for players who reflexively grab all mushrooms. Teaches skepticism of 'safe' objects.

backward_warp.description: Some warp zones send the player to an earlier world rather than a later one.
backward_warp.notes: A cruel reversal of the warp zone power from SMB1. The same mechanic weaponized against the player's expectations.

notes: Lost Levels was rejected by Nintendo of America for North American release because it was considered too difficult for Western markets.
The game features 9 worlds (Worlds 1-8 + World 9, plus hidden Worlds A-D in later game loops).
Enemy placement is more aggressive than SMB1. Enemies are placed to punish standard SMB1 movement patterns.
Some levels include moving platforms that require precise timing — Luigi's slipperiness makes these especially treacherous.
The game is primarily a 'hard mode' version of SMB1's engine and concepts.

design_notes_for_giants_drink: The Lost Levels demonstrates 'difficulty as content' — the game's primary offering is a harder version of a known thing. For players who had mastered SMB1, the engine was familiar but the execution demands were higher. This is a pure skill test against a known standard.

Luigi's different physics create a meaningful character choice from a single physics parameter change: reduce friction and increase jump. The interaction of these two changes produces an entirely different play feel — a character who can reach places Mario can't but who is harder to control precisely.

The invisible wind mechanic is a lesson in environmental physics. The player's learned intuition ('a 4-tile run-jump always clears this gap') breaks down when wind changes the constants. The environment becomes untrustworthy, which creates a different cognitive state than standard platforming.

The poison mushroom is a perfect example of 'subverted affordance.' The mushroom shape is an established signal: safe, beneficial, grab it. The poison mushroom uses that established trust against the player. Simple, devastating, and elegant as a difficulty tool.

The backward warp is similarly a subverted expectation — warp zones have always been helpful shortcuts. Using the same mechanic to punish exploration creates a paranoia about shortcuts that changes how players interact with secret paths.
